content.text: are data a component receives from the outside — set by its parent and read-only inside the component.
is data a component manages for itself — created, read, and updated internally.
If the data belongs to someone else, it is a prop. If the data belongs to this component, it is state.

attrs.alt: Side-by-side comparison of props (external, read-only, parent-controlled) vs state (internal, mutable, self-controlled)

Most real components use props and state together. Props configure; state tracks interaction.

Ask these questions when a piece of data first appears in your design:
Does the data come from a parent component?
Does the component need to change it in response to user interaction or async events?
Can it be computed from existing props or state?
→ derive it inline; don't store it separately.
Does it need to be shared with a sibling?
→ lift it to their common parent as state, then pass it down as props.
Derived Values: Neither Prop Nor State
A common beginner mistake is storing derived values in state. Anything that can be calculated from existing props or state should be computed during render — not stored.
Storing derived values in state creates two sources of truth that can get out of sync, and requires extra
logic to keep them in sync. Compute instead.

Sometimes a component needs a default value from a prop but then manages it as its own state thereafter. This is a legitimate pattern as long as you understand the implication: the initial prop is only used
signals that the prop seeds initial state but is not kept in sync with state after that. This convention is important: it communicates the contract to callers.
The props-vs-state distinction maps directly onto controlled vs uncontrolled components.
Controlled components are fully driven by props (and parent state); uncontrolled components own their own state (either in local
or the DOM). The React documentation recommends controlled components when you need to validate input, derive other data from it, or submit it through a form library.
State Belongs to an Instance, Not a Component Type
Each React component instance has its own independent state. Rendering
twice gives you two entirely separate counters, each starting from zero.
This is in contrast to props, where the same data can be sent to many components:
Storing a derived value in state
State and props disagree; extra useEffect needed
Compute the value during render; remove the state
Parent's data changes without a re-render; silent bugs
Use a callback prop to notify the parent; produce new objects
Unrelated components re-render on every local change
Keep state in the lowest component that needs it
Parent tracks things that only the child cares about
If only the child uses the data, keep it as local state
Tests should verify props-controlled output and state transitions independently.

Prop Drilling and Lifting State Up
Understanding the props-vs-state distinction immediately explains two common patterns:
. Both emerge from the fact that state is private to the component that owns it, while props are the only way to share that data.
happens when intermediate components pass props through without using them — just to get data deeper in the tree:

Prop drilling is not inherently wrong — it makes data flow explicit and easy to trace. It only becomes painful when the tree is very deep or changes frequently. Solutions include React Context, state management libraries, or component composition.
is the inverse: when two sibling components need to share the same piece of data, move the state to their nearest common ancestor and pass it down as props:
Lifting state up converts local state into shared props. The parent now owns the truth; children receive it and emit events via callbacks.
When State Changes, Props Stay in Sync

A subtle but important detail: when a parent's state changes and it re-renders, every child receives fresh props. The child does not cache the old values — props are recalculated on each render from the parent's current state.
This automatic propagation is what makes React's data flow predictable: state changes in one place ripple down through props to every descendant that cares.
Confusing props and state leads to some recurring bugs. Here are the most common anti-patterns and their fixes:
silently breaks the one-way flow and causes stale-data bugs. If you need to modify the value, derive a local variable or lift a change up via a callback.
Redundant state that mirrors a prop.
) creates two sources of truth. When the parent passes a new prop, the state retains the old value. Only do this for intentional uncontrolled patterns, and name the prop
If a value can be computed from props or other state, compute it inline:
. Storing it in state means you have to keep the state in sync manually — error-prone and unnecessary.
Using state for values that don't affect rendering.
) is the right tool for mutable values like timers, animation frames, or DOM node references that don't need to trigger re-renders.
When you are unsure whether to reach for a prop or a piece of state, run through this checklist:
Does the parent need to control this value?
Does the value change due to user interaction or async work?
Yes → state (owned by the component that handles it).
Can the value be computed from existing props/state?
Yes → lift state to common ancestor, pass down as props.
Is the value needed for rendering?
No → useRef or a plain variable, not state.

Derived values should be computed during render, not stored in state.

Keep state as low in the tree as possible; lift it only when siblings need to share it.
